html:
Piaggio & C. SpA / PGGCY

Piaggio is not the clean, debt-free compounder implied by older writeups. It is better understood as a levered, underfollowed recovery option on three things that are already visible: India commercial vehicles, premium two-wheeler mix, and a normalization of cash conversion after a rough 2025.

The headline still looks bad. FY 2025 revenue fell to €1.50 billion, EBITDA fell to €250.8 million, net profit was only €34 million, and net financial debt rose to €577.6 million. Q1 2026 revenue was down 7.8% reported and net profit fell 39.5%. That is why the stock trades near €1.72 in Milan, or roughly $8.00 per PGGCY ADR using the 4 ordinary shares per ADR ratio and current EUR/USD.

The variant view is that the bad headline is now lagging the business. In Q1 2026, revenue was only down 1.6% excluding FX, vehicle volumes rose 1.5%, gross margin reached 31.6%, and EBITDA margin was 16.8%, one of Piaggio’s best ever. Cash absorption was also much better: net debt increased by only €19 million seasonally versus €59 million a year earlier. Management then said April volumes grew close to 20% at group level, with positive contribution from all geographies. That matters because Q1 sell-in was intentionally held back ahead of new launches, so the May 2026 print is not a stale “wait for recovery” story. It is the first hard evidence that 2025 was trough-ish.

The most important piece is India commercial vehicles. Piaggio’s Q1 commercial vehicle volumes rose 28.9%, revenue rose 20.4% at constant exchange rates, and Indian electric three-wheelers grew 59.8%. This is exactly where the market should pay for torque: last-mile passenger and cargo mobility, formalization, replacement demand, and electrification. The stock is still labeled as an Italian Vespa maker, but the incremental growth engine is a small commercial mobility franchise in India with a distribution and service network that many EV challengers cannot quickly replicate.

The second piece is pricing power. Piaggio is refusing to chase Chinese scooter pricing. That sounds dangerous in a commodity vehicle segment, but Piaggio is not trying to win the commodity segment. Vespa, Aprilia, and Moto Guzzi are lifestyle, sport, and heritage brands. The company held or expanded gross margin even while reported revenue was weak, and Q1 management explicitly pointed to pricing discipline, enriched mix, and operating efficiency. The new Vespa Primavera/Sprint, Vespa 80th anniversary editions, SR GT 400, Aprilia 457/660 momentum, and revamped Moto Guzzi V7 range give the company a product-cycle bridge into the stronger Q2/Q3 selling season.

The third piece is cash conversion. Capex was €182.7 million in 2024, €140.6 million in 2025, and only €24.2 million in Q1 2026 versus €39.4 million in Q1 2025. If annual capex normalizes around €100-120 million while EBITDA holds €260-280 million, equity free cash flow can move back toward €70-90 million even with interest and a high tax rate. Against a roughly €606 million equity value, that is a low-teens forward FCF yield for a branded OEM with improving mix, not a melting scooter company.

The balance sheet is the main reason this is not a layup. Net debt near €597 million is almost the same size as the equity market cap. This is not a net-cash mispricing. But liquidity looks manageable, 2026 maturities are covered, and the company has resumed capital returns in a shareholder-friendly way: the April AGM approved cancellation of 2.33 million treasury shares and a new buyback authorization up to 27 million shares for a maximum €41.5 million. At the current market cap, that authorization is meaningful.

Valuation is the setup. MarketScreener shows Piaggio at about €606 million market cap, €1.11 billion enterprise value, 12.7x 2026 P/E, 9.9x 2027 P/E, and 0.7x 2026 EV/sales. On my numbers, it is closer to 4.2-4.6x forward EBITDA if the Q2 recovery holds. That is cheap enough that the stock does not need heroic growth. It needs proof that 2025 was the trough, India remains strong, capex falls, and net debt starts moving down.

The risk is that Europe and the U.S. remain structurally weak, APAC premium demand stays soft, and Chinese EV price pressure eventually hits Vespa’s pricing umbrella harder than management expects. If EBITDA slips toward €210-220 million and debt stays near €600 million, the equity can get hit hard. Governance is another overhang: this is controlled, Italian, illiquid, and the OTC ADR is unsponsored. The U.S. listing should be treated as a convenience wrapper around the Milan ordinary, not as a liquid U.S. stock.

Still, the asymmetry is real. Piaggio has visible brand value, a recovering India commercial-vehicle leg, premium motorcycle momentum, improving gross margin, falling capex, and a buyback at depressed prices. The market is paying for a tired scooter manufacturer; the forward business is closer to a branded mobility recovery story with an India last-mile option attached.

Bear target: $4.75 per PGGCY ADR, about 41% downside. This assumes EBITDA falls to roughly €215 million, net debt stays elevated, and the market assigns only 4.5x EBITDA.

Base target: $13.50 per ADR, about 69% upside from roughly $8.00. This assumes €275 million EBITDA, €80 million forward FCF, net debt falling toward €500 million, and a 5.5x EBITDA multiple.

Bull target: $21.00 per ADR, about 163% upside. This assumes India commercial vehicles and premium motorcycles drive revenue back toward €1.8 billion, EBITDA approaches €315 million, net debt falls, and the market pays 6.5x EBITDA for a cleaner branded mobility recovery.
